speaker: interviewer
text: Number four. Mr. Leon Borovick is being interviewed. Mr. Borovick, you were talking about how you got word that [INAUDIBLE] had perished.

speaker: interviewee
text: She came to my sister-in-law. She was in the same camp, in Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did word get--

speaker: interviewee
text: And she told her that she lost her shoe. She don't have a shoe, somehow. Maybe someone stole her shoe. And she volunteered-- volunteered to the crematorium. She went to the gas chambers, [? Bashe. ?] She told her about me, too. And my brother told me that. He came.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was word passed from the women's camp to the men's camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some people worked there, some of my-- my acquaintances. My brother [INAUDIBLE] used to go-- used to sometimes work there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Work in the women's camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Used to be sent--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: All kinds of work. All kinds of work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What specifically--

speaker: interviewee
text: Some could visit, I think. I don't know. I was a very-- I was very helpless there. I was not-- Some people were capable of-- they had all kinds of things to survive. There was a business going on, a trade. Used to exchange money, change cigarettes for bread, for potatoes, for food, for cigarettes, all kind of things. I didn't do that. I didn't have to do that. But my brother did do that. He used to-- he was very practical, and that's how he helped me a lot.

speaker: interviewee
text: So some people went to the camp. How they got there, I don't know, but some electricians had privilege to go there, maybe some to repair things. But the point is-- the thing is, my brother was there a few times. That's how he met his wife. That was my sister-in-law. He met his wife in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: The camp, yeah. But I was never in the women's camp. And many years I blamed myself for not being-- for not being able to go-- for not going to the women's camp. Maybe if she had known, if she saw me, maybe it would give her courage, maybe, to survive. But I didn't. I was not-- I blamed myself for not being so capable of doing that. Some could do that. Some had connections. I didn't, though.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was dependent on my brother and [? Kaminski. ?] I was very, very-- so I just was a prisoner like this. I was straight. Some were prisoners and they-- and they did all kinds of things to survive, you know what I mean? I didn't do it because they-- I said [INAUDIBLE] the last category. They call it [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: But later when I worked in that place, I didn't work-- work at all. So I was stronger, and that's when I was in the Underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else can you tell us about the Underground movement?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all I know [INAUDIBLE]. I was there [? till ?] it was the uprising, you know, in Birkenau. The famous uprising.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think in 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were running then.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the-- tell us about the uprising.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1944-- I think it was-- I think it was on Tuesday, if I'm not mistaken. They made an uprising, the Sonderkommando people. I think they killed two drunk-- two SS men, I think, over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the Underground that you worked for in any way connected?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I heard the Underground was not for that. They said the time is not right for that. That's right. We have to wait.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a different group that you worked for?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. It was a disagreement between the Underground and the people of the Sonderkommando. But the Underground, later they accepted that. But the Sonderkommando, they said we are-- we are in danger. You are not [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: They used to change every couple of Mondays. The Sonderkommando used to kill the group and take in other people, because they were witnesses, you know what I mean? So they said, we don't have a choice. And then they-- [INAUDIBLE] an uprising.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were certain words, slogans. And they said against fascism, all kinds of slogans against Nazism. There were 20 Russians, also in the Sonderkommando. And they all ran away. They ran. And I and my brother were running, too, with the people. All of a sudden-- all of a sudden, it was a chaos. SS couldn't control us.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were running where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through the-- no that-- where the guards, they-- what do you call this? This--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where the guards stood?

speaker: interviewee
text: The [INAUDIBLE], where they used to guard us, little-- little towers. And we went there, and the guards drew their weapons. They were afraid. They thought maybe we will take over the whole camp, the prisoners. It was a revolution. It was an uprising.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have ammunition? They had guns?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Sonderkommando? No. I don't think they had-- we didn't have any ammunition. But in the crematorium, during the day or night, there were a few [? drummers ?] there. It was not the army, but just a few [? drummers. ?] But the main leaders were killed, the Germans, so the people run. There were [? out of ?] control. We were running, too. It was like a chaos there. While running, [INAUDIBLE] we saw behind us the German-- the German-- the SS men on a motorcycle. So my brother and I, we saw a ditch that we walked. It was excrement that was-- there, hiding there. During that time, we saw they were shooting the people, our people, our comrades, those that we worked with. We didn't work with them, but were nearby groups that worked nearby.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then my brother wanted to go out from the ditch. SS men with a gun. And he said, [GERMAN]? Are you Jewish? Before we answered, a kapo-- he was not even my kapo-- from a different group-- he said, he works with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now when I look back, I see that man deserved to be in list of the righteous Gentiles. He risked his life. Imagine. He didn't work with him. And then I went down, the same thing-- a revolver on my head. And he said he works with me, too. I didn't work with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I wish I [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] man saved my life. A German kapo. A German-- imagine that. And he was a good man.

speaker: interviewee
text: I never seen him beating someone. Isn't that interesting? A German kapo saved my life. What happened to you and your brother after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, we were-- that was then in 1944. We were sent to Dachau from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, in the uprising, did anybody escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what [? came ?] [? from ?] [? that. ?] Most of them, I think they were shot. I don't think they escaped because the whole army went.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you know that the Underground did not want to participate?

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard that. I never knew it directly from my superior, but I heard. You see, there were all kinds of rumors.

speaker: interviewer
text: You heard it when? While you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before that. That they're preparing for something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, who would you hear it from?

speaker: interviewee
text: From prisoners. We used to discuss it. We used to talk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these prisoners did not know that you were part of the Underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No one knew. No one except-- no-- my own brother didn't know. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anything else about the Underground that would-- that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I know that they-- they bribed-- they looked-- they arranged the escapes of people, non-Jews and Jews. Some of them did it, some didn't come back. They were [? hanged. ?] That's what I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you don't know who those people were and how they were chosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your job remain to be the messenger between the women's--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, after that I stopped.

speaker: interviewer
text: After--

speaker: interviewee
text: After the uprising.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the uprising. What made you stop?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. It was different. No one came to me, and we didn't do anything. I didn't do anything. It was just a few weeks, then we were sent to the-- I think to [? mines ?] or something like that. We went to Dachau. We were sent from Birkenau to Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, who was we? Who was in your group?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and your brother were selected among-- among many others?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure. Yeah, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you-- while you were at Auschwitz, did you-- were you tattooed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you show that to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] 93323.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your brother in line with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: He has the same-- he has a number that's--

speaker: interviewee
text: One number maybe--

speaker: interviewer
text: One number apart?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Apart.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did that mean to you to have a tattoo put on you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Interesting. I'm not aware of that. Interesting. During all my life, people look at that, they get a little bit shocked in a way. But to me, [INAUDIBLE] my sister-in-law did away with that. She made an operation. She underwent-- she had a [INAUDIBLE]. She didn't want to-- they used to look at her in astonishment. But there's a scar still. I didn't want to take off this. In a way, I wanted to-- I want to have it. I want it. I don't know why, but [? that ?] [? is-- ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever practice Judaism in the camp? Did you say prayers or--?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. In the camp? No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say prayers?

speaker: interviewee
text: I read about Jewish people saying-- celebrating the Passover. I don't understand-- I never-- I never seen it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I read in the Jewish press about that, but I had never seen it. Who thought about this? It was a matter of life and death every minute. You didn't think of it. We didn't-- But some, I read about that. [? Basically, ?] they managed to eat matzo. I don't know why. And maybe they're exaggerating. They say they were saying [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. You know the song? I never heard that. And I think they're exaggerating. I was not [INAUDIBLE]. What was the talk among men in the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: All kind of things. Even in Auschwitz we sang a little. We sang a lot in Auschwitz. There were learned songs in Auschwitz. I told you, I learned that one song.

speaker: interviewer
text: What song?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two songs.

speaker: interviewer
text: What songs?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I used to play for the people in Auschwitz. One of my [INAUDIBLE] workers taught me that. We were sitting in the barrack at night. Sometimes we used to sing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the song you learned in Auschwitz? Could you sing it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I'll try. [CLEARS THROAT] I [INAUDIBLE] in Auschwitz. And then I came to America. I heard the same melody, different words. A ghetto song. But I heard it in Auschwitz with different words. And goes like this. [CLEARS THROAT]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where's my mandolin? [INAUDIBLE] I'll see if I'll be able to do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PLAYING MANDOLIN] Hold on. I have to-- [TUNING MANDOLIN] [PLAYING MANDOLIN] [SINGING IN YIDDISH] Try to remember the words. [SINGING IN YIDDISH] [? I got the ?] [? words. ?] [? It's ?] [? coming. ?] [SINGING IN YIDDISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Did you feel proud of being part of the Underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very proud. At the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very proud. I knew I did something which to me was sacred. A sacred thing. We worked-- we were fighting the Germans, and I was the only one. [INAUDIBLE] and I can't-- I don't understand how I volunteered without asking any questions. I risked my life. That is [? the thing ?]. That was twice being a prisoner, but death was hovering upon you. And then in addition, I had to be careful [INAUDIBLE] to deliver that to [INAUDIBLE] [? area. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us now about the transport to Dachau.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before that we went to Sachsenhausen. It was a couple of days. It was very clean there, very nice. We were not beaten. It was good there. Then we went to Dachau. The last half a year-- half a year before the liberation, we were in Dachau most of the time. In Dachau I was never beaten there. We worked hard there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And over there in Dachau was something that the world doesn't know. There was a literary group. Can you imagine this? In a concentration camp, Dachau.

speaker: interviewee
text: You will hear about people tell about Dachau. Many people died there-- that's true, too. Many people. Before I came there, Dachau was a very famous camp. The German communists were sent there by Hitler. But I don't-- compared to Auschwitz, it was a paradise. We worked very hard, and the last couple of months, the regime was milder because the war was-- was going to be finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Yiddish-- the Jewish kapo let us gather. We used to gather together in that camp, and we used to do literary work. We used to read poems. You had books?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were a few poets there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have books?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It remembered by heart. And we wrote. I wrote a few poems. I remember a few words. I remember-- oh my goodness, if I could remember. A very optimistic poem I wrote, I remember, in Dachau. Maybe later I'll remember. And we used to gather there. The leader of the group was a man by the name of [? Wygodzki, ?] Stanislaw [? Wygodzki. ?] He was a Polish poet-- a Jew, but a Polish poet. A great intellectual. And he used write. People wrote essays and poems, and some people just were reciting poems [INAUDIBLE]. We used to gather, and there were guards standing and watching, the SS [INAUDIBLE]. And this kapo gave us soup for that. Imagine that. And we wrote and we discussed literature. We discussed the value of the poems. Once I wrote a poem, a lousy poem, they criticized me.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHTER] What was the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember-- disgusted me. In Dachau. [? That ?] [? means ?] people say the Germans-- they didn't dehumanize us, see? We were superior. We were the ones with-- with a conscience, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] with values. But that is not known. You'll never hear about the literary group. That was in Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the nature-- what was the content of the poetry? You said yours was optimistic.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very optimistic.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were mostly--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, I remember. [SPEAKING YIDDISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: The day is coming, a new dawn is-- [LAUGHS] rising. Very optimistic. Where did that come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did that come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember the second [INAUDIBLE] at first was that we were hardened in Auschwitz in [INAUDIBLE]. Our spirit is alert. We are alert. [LAUGHS] The rest, I don't-- I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have access to paper and pencil, or was everything--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I don't know how, but I wrote. In fact, I wrote this poem down. When I read that for the people. We were gathered there, a few of the prisoners who came. It was known that is a group [INAUDIBLE] will be there inside the camp. They came like a meeting that we brought there, and we were all reciting, the people. Then we came back and we analyzed that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it human spirit or was there something intrinsically Jewish about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not specifically Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was just human?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. My poem was not at all about Jewish people. Just the dawn comes, and some-- a new dawn comes, and we are ready for--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your brother part of this group?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. A different type. My brother and I were two different-- diametrically different types. That's why I survived. Because he was the one to manage in Auschwitz, you see. I wasn't able to manage, you see. You have to know all kind of tricks to survive. I didn't [INAUDIBLE]. Like, to change-- you had a portion to change for-- the bread for soup or the soup for cigarettes. There were cigarettes there. They got a trade going, was going on there. You had to have a good group to work somehow.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, well, these people saved my life, a few people. Polish-- Polish men. He was a murderer, but he saved my life. That's interesting. I even seen him beating people to death. But I think the way he told me that I know-- when I came there, he was already an old [? kapo, ?] an old [? [GERMAN] ?]. But he told me in my face-- told me, I would have killed you. But I didn't think of it. Imagine-- I was running from-- who did it? I was the only one.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sure I was the only one out of the hundreds of thousands who survived. To run away from the group, that was against the rules. A crime. It was punished to death.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think that's so?

speaker: interviewee
text: Young. I was young. I don't know. I was courageous then. That took courage, of course. Of course, I don't know. I didn't even think that I'm risking something. I risked not my life-- [INAUDIBLE] tortured. Worse than die-- to die. They tortured you to death. It was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, how long were you in Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: About six months there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you did no work there, or you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Work. All kind of work, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Remember used to carry cement or something. Labor, hard labor, yeah. But we were glad to do-- I didn't [INAUDIBLE] any beatings, you know what I mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there more food or less food than before?

speaker: interviewee
text: More than Auschwitz because I remember [INAUDIBLE] six months [INAUDIBLE], we didn't have any other income. In Auschwitz we had income. My brother used to trade these things. Somehow he managed. But we survived with a portion. As I said, we were young. Later, elderly people wouldn't be able to survive on this portion.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you know by this time that you had left-- the only person leff-- the only family person left in your life was your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew that by now. And did you get any word of what was going on outside in the war, outside of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We had meetings and they told us about the partisans, about the people--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean you had meetings?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the Underground people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Underground in Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think with that men that-- I think [? Munk. ?] No, in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Dachau we didn't know anything. We didn't know about anything. We just knew the war is ending, that the war is-- because their regime was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: How did [? Munk ?] get his information?

speaker: interviewee
text: We don't know about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't know. Was there a name to that-- [? LB: Munk ?] criticized me. He criticized me. In that uprising [INAUDIBLE]. He says why didn't I die with those who were shot? [LAUGHS] He was a fanatic, you see. Why didn't you die? That was revolution-- calls you to die. You had to die. But he didn't die.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I should have told him, why didn't you? Why did you survive?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that why you stopped--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, then-- no one-- see, everything was changed. There was no more sending to the crematoriums. No gas chambers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that uprising.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: People weren't-- you heard about Himmler? They made a deal with someone that they'll stop sending, and they stopped. [INAUDIBLE] too much, I think. There was no--

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you saying that Himmler made an agreement with the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I read about that, that the [? last ?] [? couple-- ?] even from the Red Cross, we got food the last few-- you remember in Dachau, we had food.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was different, see? We had food from the Red Cross. Remember, we had sugar. Some people died from-- they ate sugar too much. It affected their stomach. How do you know it came from the Red Cross?

speaker: interviewee
text: They told-- we knew. They told us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We talked about that. I don't know, but people talked. They heard that from the Red Cross. And I read that, too, after the war, about--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it hard to separate those things that you heard after the war from those things that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Sometimes there are contradictions. I don't know what to believe. I don't know. But the point is, now in my mind, there's that rumor that was from the Red Cross. I don't know how-- if I-- if I-- if I talked about that in Dachau, or maybe after the war. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there many rumors that circulated among the prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was in the air that the war was-- that they're going to lose the war because we were [INAUDIBLE] the march. The death march. I was-- I was in that march, I and my brother. But they evacuated us because the [? American ?] army, the Allies came already. They were near Berlin. We knew that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew that.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you started on the death march, you knew that--

speaker: interviewee
text: We're on the road, we know because they evacuated the guards, because-- they evacuated the camp and themselves. They couldn't stay. It was near the front. The front is-- we knew that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the death march like? How many men did they march?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember-- we were-- it was-- what do you call that thing in English when you take the horse and you travel on that thing with the horses, with two horses?

speaker: interviewer
text: The horses were pulling a wagon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. A wagon, yeah. So I think we rode on the wagons. We were-- if it was the horses, I don't know. Maybe it was trucks. I can't-- it's chaos in my mind. I just know that we were on the road. And they were bombarding there. There were bombarding it. We used to hide. We used to lie down on the ground.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you marched?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just five in line, like this, like soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still marching with your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My brother was all this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Next to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next to me all the-- all these years. All these--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have to bribe people to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was also a unique thing. My brother-- that is something. Every camp he was with me. We were sleeping on the same boards and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anybody who tried to separate you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We had another-- we had two brothers there, I remember, my stepbrothers. I had four stepbrothers. One was in America already, the oldest one. He took us over, sent out the visa. He was in Philadelphia during the war. And the other brothers, one of them, [? Hirshke, ?] was not in the camp. He was sent to the crematorium, I think, with his wife. And there was two brothers, [? Shlemke ?] and [? Awremel. ?] They were wonderful people, beautiful people. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] The youngest one was [? Shlemke. ?] We were walking, my brother and I, and [? Shlemke ?] joined us, three. I remember I used to take the bread, biggest portions. [? Shlemke ?] used to take the-- for all three portions, he used to keep it. Then he used to-- used to give us at work. He kept the bread. But it was [INAUDIBLE]-- It was in different barracks, somehow, than [? it used ?] [? to ?] [? be. ?] He was sent to Auschwitz at a different time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remember he was sick, and he died. My brother used to visit him every day, and he died. He was in the [? crematoriums. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: And one brother, [? Awremel, ?] he died-- that was-- the whole barrack [INAUDIBLE] was sent to the crematorium for nothing. There was something, a unique thing. They took the whole block. The people they sent-- he was with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But on the death march, on the march from--

speaker: interviewee
text: On the death march, we know that we went from place to place. We were, I think, in one place called [? Allach, ?] I think, if I'm not mistaken. Stayed for a few days.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you marching?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. About two weeks or something. I don't know. It was the [? famous ?] death march.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many out of those that started--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know exactly how--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of those that started died along the way, would you guess?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Many people died. I just remember that I used to-- my brother and I used to cling to each other to get warm. I think it was cold because in evening, at night, we used to lie on the floor. [? with ?] the bombing. [INAUDIBLE] bomb the places.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you given food along the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know really how we got food. Did they give us? I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you dressed in prison uniforms?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. I don't how we got the food. Interesting. I don't remember. [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the end of tape number four.

speaker: interviewee
text: Still to go one more, huh?
